coredata.dc:title: Multimodal applications of green carbon dots derived from Potentilla indica (mock strawberry): Antioxidant, antimicrobial, and quenching based quercetin sensor

coredata.dc:description: This study utilized a simple hydrothermal technique to synthesize negatively charged carbon dots from Potentilla indica fruit (MSTCDs) with antioxidant and broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, as well as a specific capacity to detect quercetin. The characterization of MSTCDs was performed using spectrophotometry (UV–vis, fluorescence, X-ray photoelectron, and Fourier-transform infrared), zeta potential, and transmission electron microscopy. The prepared MSTCDs had an average size of 8 ± 0.22 nm and zeta potential of −21 mV. The prepared MSTCDs showed negligible cytotoxicity and were found to be biocompatible with human microglial cells. MSTCDs used the static quenching and inner filter effect as their two sensing mechanisms to detect quercetin. The fluorescence intensity of the MSTCDs decreased as the dose of quercetin increased, showing a linear correlation within the dose range of 0.01–70 μM (R2 = 0.9980) and the lowest detection limit of 2 nM. The prepared MSTCDs were applied to detect quercetin in the orange and grape juice, yielding values of 3.27 and 7.04 μM, respectively. Further, the MSTCDs displayed antioxidant activity by scavenging 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl, and hydroxyl radicals, with an EC50 value of 9.3, 140.82, and 187.91 µg/mL, respectively. The antiradical activity of MSTCDs was slightly lower than the ascorbic acid except for ABTS radicals, where MSTCDs showed superior scavenging ability (EC50 = 9.3 µg/mL) compared to the control (EC50 = 33.7 µg/mL). MSTCDs exhibited dose-dependent antimicrobial activity against fungi, Gram-negative bacteria, and Gram-positive bacteria. Thus, the developed CDs showed potential as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and quenching-based quercetin sensor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study utilized a simple hydrothermal technique to synthesize negatively charged carbon dots from Potentilla indica fruit (MSTCDs) with antioxidant and broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, as well as a specific capacity to detect quercetin. The characterization of MSTCDs was performed using spectrophotometry (UV–vis, fluorescence, X-ray photoelectron, and Fourier-transform infrared), zeta potential, and transmission electron microscopy. The prepared MSTCDs had an average size of 8 ± 0.22 nm and zeta potential of −21 mV. The prepared MSTCDs showed negligible cytotoxicity and were found to be biocompatible with human microglial cells. MSTCDs used the static quenching and inner filter effect as their two sensing mechanisms to detect quercetin. The fluorescence intensity of the MSTCDs decreased as the dose of quercetin increased, showing a linear correlation within the dose range of 0.01–70 μM (R2 = 0.9980) and the lowest detection limit of 2 nM. The prepared MSTCDs were applied to detect quercetin in the orange and grape juice, yielding values of 3.27 and 7.04 μM, respectively. Further, the MSTCDs displayed antioxidant activity by scavenging 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl, and hydroxyl radicals, with an EC50 value of 9.3, 140.82, and 187.91 µg/mL, respectively. The antiradical activity of MSTCDs was slightly lower than the ascorbic acid except for ABTS radicals, where MSTCDs showed superior scavenging ability (EC50 = 9.3 µg/mL) compared to the control (EC50 = 33.7 µg/mL). MSTCDs exhibited dose-dependent antimicrobial activity against fungi, Gram-negative bacteria, and Gram-positive bacteria. Thus, the developed CDs showed potential as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and quenching-based quercetin sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles derived from metal-based compounds and chemical precursors possess inherent toxicity. To address this concern, scientists are actively working on producing nanoparticles based on green chemistry principles [1]. Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) with sizes less than 10 nm have been used in a range of areas, broadly classified as nanomedicine, cosmetics, food, energy, electronics, and the environment [2,3]. These green synthesized nanocarbons are becoming more and more popular, and the use of CDs for various applications has increased due to their beneficial properties. These include water solubility, inherent fluorescence, upconversion, biocompatibility, simple synthesis and modification, thermal and optical stability, and low toxicity [4,5].

CDs benefit from heteroatom doping with elements such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron, and phosphorous, which improves their fluorescence properties, tunable band gap, and physiochemical features [6]. It could be due to differences in the proportion and electronegativity of doping elements, which cause defects in CDs’ structure and increase the number of active sites on CDs. Thus, doped CDs can be utilized for a variety of applications [7]. Information on naturally S doped CDs is limited. The inclusion of S atoms leads to the alteration of the electronic structure of the CDs, providing emissive traps and energy states for capturing photoexcited electrons [8].

Numerous studies have reported the synthesis of CDs from biomass and their applicability in various domains [9]. Nevertheless, there is untapped potential for studying the use of plant components with sustainable biomass and medicinal properties in the field of nanotechnology. One often overlooked plant is Potentilla indica, which is native to eastern and southern Asia. A member of the Rosaceae family, this plant is commonly known as mock strawberry (MST) and contains a variety of phytochemicals, such as volatile oils, sterols, ellagic acid, triterpenes, and phenolic compounds such as anthocyanins [10]. Earlier research has documented the beneficial effects of its extracts and phytochemicals, such as antitumor, antimicrobial, and antioxidant [11–13]. Asian medicine often relies on the use of Potentilla indica to address various health concerns, such as swelling of the tissues, diabetes, malignancies, inborn fever, and leprosy [14].

According to molecular biology research, an abundance of free radicals can lead to harmful oxidative processes within the body, which have been linked to the development of degenerative diseases [15]. A comprehensive investigation has been conducted to evaluate the antioxidant characteristics of botanical extracts and metal nanoparticles derived from plant extracts, with the aim of eliminating these radicals [16]. Biomass-derived CDs have demonstrated remarkable antiradical properties among nanoparticles [17].

Antimicrobial resistance presents a substantial risk to the well-being of people of all ages as a result of the widespread use and misuse of antimicrobial drugs. Therefore, the development of novel antimicrobial agents will have the potential to overcome microbial multidrug resistance [18]. CDs offer a number of benefits over the commonly used antibiotics, such as high specificity, superior photo-stability, and reduced cytotoxicity. Furthermore, researchers discovered that the heteroatom-doped CDs possess exceptional antimicrobial properties [19].

Quercetin is an aglycone-type flavonoid glycoside produced from plants [20]. It functions as a therapeutic agent by treating oxidative stress, heart problems, cancer, inflammation, and lipid peroxidation [21]. Although quercetin is normally considered safe according to the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), it can lead to migraine [22], renal dysfunction, and enhanced tumor growth when used for an extended period of time [23]. Hence, determining the presence of quercetin in various foods, medications, and biological samples is of the utmost importance. Quercetin has been detected previously using conventional approaches, including high pressure liquid chromatography [24], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [25], and ultraviolet (UV) spectrophotometry [26]. These approaches, however, necessitate complex pre-treatment procedures and the expertize of trained manpower. To overcome these problems, fluorescence-based method is a good choice due to its cleanliness, simplicity, and accuracy. Previous studies have documented the detection of quercetin using CDs derived from a variety of chemical sources [27–33]. Recently, researchers reported the detection of quercetin using CDs derived from Coptis chinensis and corn stover [34,35]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop fluorescence-based sensors that are sensitive, safe, and environmentally friendly in order to detect quercetin.

In this study, we prepared CDs from an aqueous fruit extract of Potentilla indica, also known as mock strawberry (hereinafter referred to as MSTCDs) by utilizing the common hydrothermal approach. This work is the first to report the synthesis of MSTCDs from mock strawberries and their use as antimicrobial agents, antioxidants, and nanoprobes for detecting quercetin in real orange and grape juice samples. The fluorescent MSTCDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity when tested on human microglial (HMC3) cells. Furthermore, the effects of temperature, ionic strength, pH, and UV irradiation on the stability of MSTCDs are investigated. Moreover, the plausible sensing mechanism for the selective detection of quercetin has also been described. Notably, the MSTCDs exhibited the lowest limit of detection (LOD) for quercetin when compared with the previously reported green CDs from different biomass. The antioxidant property is assessed based on scavenging of hydroxyl (OH), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and 2,2′-Azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) radicals. To study the antimicrobial properties of MSTCDs, the study used Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains, as well as fungi.

The study used fresh Potentilla indica fruits collected from the banks of a local stream in Bokjeong-dong, South Korea. Rutin, apigenin, ascorbic acid (AA), ABTS, DPPH, ethanol, potassium persulfate (K₂S₂O₈), salicylic acid, iron chloride, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), quercetin, quinine sulfate, cobalt nitrate, sodium carbonate, potassium chloride, barium hydroxide, uric acid, gallic acid, tannic acid, iron oxide, mercury chloride, copper nitrate, tetracycline, and penicillin were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Muller-Hinton (MH) broth and MH agar were acquired from Difco (Becton, Dickinson and Company), USA. The sterile swabs and Digimatic vernier calliper were purchased from Dong-A Healthcare Company and Mitutoyo Corporation Caliper (Japan), respectively. For the biocompatibility study, a cell cytotoxicity assay (CCK)-8 kit was used that was purchased from Dojindo Molecular Technologies, Rockville, MD, USA. Orange and grape juices were obtained from the local market. The solutions were prepared using deionized water that was generated using Milli-Q water equipment manufactured by Samwoo S&T Co. Ltd. All compounds were of high purity and used without additional purification.

In the first step, 50 g of Potentilla indica fruits were rinsed with double distilled water and ground into pulp with 50 mL of distilled water using a mixer grinder. The pulp was then sieved to obtain juice and thermally treated in an autoclave kept at 180 °C for 16 h. During the final stage, we removed large particles from the solution by centrifuging it for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The residual liquid obtained after filtration through a 0.22 µm syringe was lyophilized and stored at room temperature for future use.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of MSTCDs were recorded using the Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (USA) and the Varian Cary 50 CA UV–vis spectrophotometer (USA), respectively. The study used a transmission electron microscope (TEM), FEI Titan 80–300, USA to record the nanostructure and dimensions of MSTCDs. The Jasco-4600 fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer was used to obtain FTIR data. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Multilab-2000) with Al K Alpha was utilized for elemental analysis. The Nanosizer 500, Anton Paar, was used to obtain the zeta potential of MSTCDs. The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by a fluorescence lifetime analyser from instrument Photon Technology International (PTI) (Easy Life System).

The calculation of QY was done using the method with standard quinine sulfate (54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4). Following is the equation used for the determination of QY [36].
(1) QMSTCD = QSTD (IMSTCD/ISTD) (ASTD/AMSTCD) (ղMSTCD/ղSTD)2
Where ‘MSTCD’, ‘STD’, and ‘Q’ refer to MSTCDs, quinine sulfate, and QY, respectively. ‘I’ is the fluorescence intensity, and ‘A’ denotes the absorbance. ‘ղ’ stands for the refractive index of the solvents. To reduce inner filter effects, the absorbance of the MSTCDs and quinine sulfate was retained below 0.10 at a wavelength of 320 nm.

To evaluate the antioxidant potential of MSTCDs, an ABTS assay was conducted. The ABTS+ radical was made by mixing an aqueous solution of 7 mM ABTS with aqueous K₂S₂O₈ (2.4 mM) in a ratio of 1:0.5 (v/v) and held in the dark for 16 h to allow the reaction to occur. Thereafter, the absorbance of the obtained solution was adjusted to around 0.72 ± 0.02 a.u. at 734 nm. The test (MSTCDs) and the control (AA) were added to the diluted ABTS+ in a ratio of 1:9. Subsequently, the mixture was placed in the dark at an ambient (25 °C) temperature for a duration of 10 min. The UV spectra were measured at 734 nm. The following equation was employed to determine the ABTS+ scavenging percentage of MSTCDs:
(2) % ABTS scavenging = (Acl−Asl)/Act *100%

The representation of absorbances in the absence (control) and presence of a sample or standard is expressed as Acl and Asl, respectively. The graph of percentage ABTS scavenging vs. standard or MSTCD concentrations (μg/mL) was plotted to calculate the EC50 value (the dose of antioxidants needed to suppress antioxidants by half) of the standard or MSTCDs for scavenging ABTS+ radicals.

The study performed the DPPH assay in the dark to evaluate the antiradical activity of MSTCDs. A 1:9 ratio of different sample (MSTCDs or standard) concentrations to methanolic DPPH (100 µM) was added and reacted for 20 min. Following this, we measured the absorbance at 517 nm and used the following equation to obtain the results:
(3) % DPPH scavenging = (Acl−Asl)/Act *100%

The representation of DPPH absorbances in the absence (control) and presence of a sample or standard is expressed as Acl and Asl, respectively. To find the EC50 value of MSTCDs for removing DPPH, graphs were created showing the percentage of radical scavenging against the doses of the MSTCDs (μg/mL).

The OH radical scavenging ability of MSTCDs was tested using the Fenton reaction [17]. Initially, OH radicals were produced by combining 1.8 mM of ethanolic salicylic acid (375 µL) with 1.8 mM of aqueous iron chloride (500 µL). The MSTCDs or standard at various concentrations (μg/mL) were mixed with OH radicals. Next, 25 µL of H2O2 (100 mM) was added, and the mixture was reacted for 10 minutes. After incubation, the reaction mixture was centrifuged for 3 min at 5000 rpm, and at 510 nm, the absorbance of the supernatant was calculated. The scavenging% was calculated as follows:
(4) % OH scavenging = (Acl−Asl)/Act *100%
where OH absorbance in the absence (control) and presence of a MSTCDs or standard are represented as Acl and Asl, respectively. The EC50 value of the MSTCDs or standard for OH radical scavenging was determined by analysing the relationship between the percentage of radical scavenging vs. concentration of the MSTCDs or standard (μg/mL).

The CCK-8 assay protocol as recommended by the manufacturer, was used to test the cytotoxicity of MSTCDs on HMC3 cells. HMC3 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells/well) in DMEM and allowed to adhere and grow for 24 h at 37 °C in a CO2 (5 %) incubator. Following 24 h post-stabilization, the cells were incubated with 100 μL of media containing different concentrations of MSTCDs (1.95, 3.90, 7.81, 15.62, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/mL) for an additional 24 h. The next day, MSTCDs-treated media was removed, and cells were exposed to 100 μL of DMEM supplemented with 10 μL of CCK for about 2 h. The absorbance was quantified at 450 nm using a microplate reader manufactured by BioTek Inc., Winooski, VT, USA.

The study investigated the antibacterial and antifungal properties of the as-prepared MSTCDs using the agar-well diffusion technique. The model organisms consisted of three bacterial strains (Staphylococcus (S.) aureus, Bacillus (B.) subtilis, and Escherichia (E.) coli) and one fungal strain (Candida (C.) albicans). The MH agar plates were inoculated with overnight grown cultures, and 7 mm diameter wells were created using a borer. The MSTCDs at a concentration of 1 mg/mL were added to wells in varying volumes (25, 50, 75, and 100 µL). Plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C for bacteria and 30 °C for fungus. A vernier calliper was used to precisely measure the diameters of the inhibition zones. Tetracycline and penicillin were used as positive controls, for the bacterial and fungal strains, respectively. The autoclaved distilled water served as a negative control. A total of three well diffusion experiments were performed.

The quercetin detection involved adding different amounts of quercetin (ranging from 0 to 200 µM) to 2 mL of MSTCDs. The total amount was set to 2.5 mL by adding distilled water. In the presence of potential interferents (rutin, apigenin, cobalt nitrate, sodium carbonate, potassium chloride, potassium sulfate, AA, potassium iodide, barium hydroxide, uric acid, gallic acid, tannic acid, iron oxide, mercury chloride, and copper nitrate), the change in the fluorescence intensity of MSTCDs was evaluated to establish their selectivity.

Orange and grape juices were filtered by a 0.22 µM filter. These juices were diluted 100 times and spiked with different concentrations of quercetin (1, 20, and 60 µM). Tap water was also infused with an equal amount of quercetin. Using the conventional method of recovery through addition, a study was conducted on the suitability of MSTCDs for detecting quercetin in real samples using fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, we assessed the naturally occurring quercetin content in orange and grape juices without diluting them 100 times.

The synthesis of blue, fluorescent MSTCDs was achieved using a hydrothermal approach from mock strawberries ( Scheme 1). The dimensions of the MSTCDs were observed by analysing them through TEM, which revealed their uniform and spherical morphology ( Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM in the inset of Fig. 1a displayed a 0.24 nm lattice spacing. Using image J software, the average particle size was found to be 8 ± 0.22 nm, and the size distribution ranged from 4 to 11 nm (n = 50) as shown in Fig. 1b.

The chemical composition and surface functionalities of MSTCDs were characterized using FT-IR spectra. Fig. 1c showed the presence of various functional groups on MSTCDs. The region above 2000 cm−1 refers to triple bonds, 1638 cm−1 refers to CO stretching vibration [37], 1394 cm−1 represents C–N stretching vibrations [38], 1246.3 cm−1 belongs to CC stretching vibrations [39], and 1058.8 cm−1 refers to C–O/SO stretching vibrations. The absorption peaks at 2901.1 and 2981.7 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H indicating the presence of amine groups. The band at 3343.9 cm−1 relates to O–H stretching [40]. These findings revealed that the surface of MSTCDs exhibited a significant amount of carbonyl, hydroxyl, amine, and other functional groups.

XPS revealed more details about the elemental makeup of the produced MSTCDs. Fig. 2a illustrates that MSTCDs mainly consist of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), N1s, and S2p. Moreover, the details of the chemical bonds of synthesized MSTCDs were further investigated through core-level spectra. After deconvolution, the spectra of C1s showed that it had functional elements for CC, C–H, C–N, C–O, and O–CO at 281.4, 283.5, 285.2, 286.7, and 287.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c displays the O1s spectrum, revealing four distinct peaks at 529 (CO), 530.5 (CO, C–OH), 531.9 (C–O), and 533.2 eV (OC–O) [41]. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d exhibited two distinct peaks at 398.43 eV and 400.29 eV, which were attributed to C–NC (pyridine N) and C–N–C (pyrrole N), respectively [42]. There were five peaks detected in the S2p spectrum, with energy values of 161.4, 163.5, 165.2, 167.5, and 167.6 eV (Fig. 2e). These peaks represent different chemical species, including S2p1/2, S2p2/3, C–S–C, C−SO2, and C–SOx (where x can be 2,3, and 4), respectively [43,44]. N has a valency of 3, which is in close proximity to carbon's valency of 4 [45]. The valency of S is 2, and its electronegativity is 2.58, comparable to the value of 2.55 for a carbon atom [46]. Hence, these atoms substantially improve the optical, electron transfer, and chemical characteristics of CDs.

The optical characteristics of the synthesized MSTCDs were investigated using UV-Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 3a displays the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of MSTCDs, revealing two absorption peaks at 327 and 280.9 nm. These peaks correspond to different types of transitions, with the first peak representing n -π* transitions of the CO and CN groups, and the second peak is attributing to π -π* transitions of the CC group, respectively [47]. The crucial factor for using CDs in practical applications is their photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. The PL spectra revealed the typical excitation-dependent emission pattern of MSTCDs, as seen in Fig. 3b. The emission spectra significantly changed when the excitation wavelength was surged from 280 to 480 nm. The observed maximum excitation and maximum emission wavelengths were noted to be 360 nm and 436.5 nm, respectively. The fluorescence QY of MSTCDs was found to be 4.3 %. Further, the surface charge of the MSTCDs was evaluated by measuring the zeta potential. It was calculated to be −21.7 mV, implying their high colloidal stability (Fig. S1). The significant negative zeta potential could be a result of the substantial presence of oxygen containing groups such as C–O, CO or O–H, onto the surface of as-synthesized CDs [48].

Photostability is an important optical feature of fluorescent CDs. Therefore, we tested the photostability of MSTCDs for 60 minutes using a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. Figure S2 shows the observed results. Interestingly, we observed no discernible differences in the PL intensity of MSTCDs before and after irradiation. Furthermore, the as-synthesized MSTCDs demonstrated remarkable stability over a wide pH range (4–14). As shown in Fig. S3, the PL intensity was rather consistent from pH 4–11, with a noticeable drop at lower (pH 3) and higher pH values (pH 12, 13, and 14). The decrease in PL intensity can be ascribed to the existence of COO- and N-H groups on the MSTCDs’ surface, which caused the formation of different dissociation constants and isoelectric points [36]. The plot of zeta potential against several pH values ranging from 3 to 13 revealed the isoelectric point for MSTCDs to be around 6 (Fig. S4). The negligible electrostatic repulsion force among CDs facilitates their aggregation at the isoelectric point. Under pH conditions below the isoelectric point, MSTCDs exhibited a positive charge, whereas at pH levels above the isoelectric point, they carried a negative charge [49]. Figure S5 shows the thermal stability of the synthesized MSTCDs at temperatures ranging from 20 to 60 °C. The study found that MSTCDs had a high PL intensity at lower temperatures, which decreased as the temperature increased. The temperature-driven movements of electrons generated by light from emissive to higher-lying nonradiative domains are associated with the drop in the PL intensity of CDs caused by rising temperature [50]. Further investigation on the impact of various ionic concentrations of NaCl solution, ranging from 0 to 2 M, on the PL of MSTCDs, showed the excellent ionic stability of the synthesized MSTCDs (Fig. S6). The PL intensity of MSTCDs remained rather constant regardless of intense salt stress, indicating that their surface functional groups were resistant to ionization. These findings indicate that as-prepared MSTCDs are suitable for optical and biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging of ABTS, DPPH and OH radicals
ce:section.ce:para: The antiradical activity of MSTCDs was assessed using ABTS, DPPH, and OH methods. All three assays used AA as the standard. The efficiency of CDs to remove radicals varies considerably depending on their structure, composition, and source material. Radical scavenging occurs by a number of different processes, such as adding radical adducts to sp2 carbon domains, electron transfer, and hydrogen transfer. These mechanisms work alone or together to scavenge radicals [51].

The ability of CDs to transfer electrons or donate hydrogen determines their antioxidant ability. The elimination percentage of ABTS+ radicals was observed to be proportional to the increase in concentrations of MSTCDs, as shown in Fig. 3c. As MSTCDs concentrations increased from 3.9 to 250 µg/mL, the scavenging activity increased from 28.55 % to 93.58 %. Upon further increasing the MSTCDs amount, the scavenging% remained almost the same. The EC50 value of the MSTCDs was observed to be 9.3 µg/mL, which was lower than the EC50 value (33.7 µg/mL) of the standard (AA). Prior research has reported that the presence of N-doped state can augment the capacity of CDs to eliminate radicals, potentially owing to the difference in electronegativity between carbon and N [52]. The excellent ABTS+ radical scavenging potential of MSTCDs could be due to the presence of surface functional groups that participated in the removal of free radicals, resulting in a stable complex in the ABTS assay [53]. A study showed that the CDs made from Lonicera caerulea (also called honeysuckle berry) had an IC50 value of 625 µg/mL for removing ABTS radicals [54].

By accepting a hydrogen radical from MSTCDs, the unstable DPPH exhibited a color change from dark violet to bright yellow, converting into a stable DPPH-H complex. DPPH radical scavenging by MSTCDs was found to be dose dependent. Increasing MSTCDs’ concentrations from 3.9 to 500 µg/mL, led to a progressive rise in antioxidant activity from 0.9 % to 84.34 % (Fig. 3d). However, the scavenging activity did not increase with MSTCDs doses above 500 µg/mL. For DPPH, the EC50 values for MSTCDs and standard were calculated to be 140.82 µg/mL and 45.44 µg/mL, respectively. The results depicted that MSTCDs had good DPPH removal abilities. Previously, the ethyl acetate extract of Potentilla indica showed a maximum DPPH scavenging activity of 24.8 % at 35 mg/mL, which was low in contrast to MSTCDs [14]. Mechanistically, an earlier study found that the presence of -NH2 and -COOH groups on the surface of carbon nanodots effectively neutralized the DPPH radicals through electron and hydrogen transfer processes, respectively [55]. Hence, it was concluded that at the nano level, the material’s antioxidant potential may be improved due to the formation of various functional groups like OH, NH2 and COOH during the thermal treatment. Ghorbani et al. found that CDs derived from baker’s yeast compounds removed DPPH with an IC50 value of 570 μg/mL [56].

Among free radicals, OH radicals are the most reactive and contains unparalleled oxidation capability [57]. The elimination of OH radicals by MSTCDs was highest (70.16 %) at 250 µg/mL with dose-dependent increment (Fig. 3e). MSTCDs showed a comparable EC50 value of 187.91 µg/mL with that of the standard (159.92 µg/mL). CDs have a high surface-to-volume ratio and the presence of reducing elements including C-S, O-H, C-N, CH, and CO, which allow them to react with electron-rich radicals such as OH [58]. Therefore, the presence of these features significantly contributed to the exceptional ability of the MSTCDs to scavenge OH radicals.

As shown in Fig. 3f, MSTCDs exhibited no toxicity towards HMC3 cells after treating at different concentrations. Higher concentrations of MSTCDs exhibited enhanced cell proliferation. Even at a high concentration of 500 μg/mL, cells retained their 100 % viability. These results indicate the MSTCDs' biocompatibility and potential for biological applications.

The current study observed the remarkable microbicidal activity of MSTCDs against three bacterial isolates (B. subtilis, S. aureus, and E. coli) and one fungal strain (C. albicans). The appearance of zones of inhibition demonstrated that MSTCDs have antibacterial and antifungal activity ( Fig. 4). As depicted in Fig. 4(a, a-1, b, b-1, and c, c-1), the well diffusion method showed a clear zone of inhibition for all bacterial strains when tested at different volumes (10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µL) of MSTCDs (1 mg/mL). Table 1 summarizes the results of the well diffusion test. For B. subtilis, S. aureus, and E. coli, the obtained diameter of zone of inhibition was 29.61 mm, 30.90 mm, and 23.88 mm at 100 µL of MSTCDs, respectively. Among all the tested bacterial strains, MSTCDs showed a better microbicidal effect against Gram-positive bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria possess a thick peptidoglycan outer cell membrane, while Gram-negative bacteria have a thin, intricate lipopolysaccharide-containing outer membrane, resulting in greater defense against antimicrobials [56]. Electrostatic forces help CQDs attach to the surface of bacterial cells, but the loose, thick membrane of Gram-positive bacteria is better suited for absorbing CQDs [59]. The antifungal ability of MSTCDs was also assessed and showed a 22.65 mm zone of inhibition at 100 µL against C. albicans (Figs. 4(d, d-1). The sensitivity of fungal and Gram-negative bacterial strains was almost the same (Table 1). MSTCDs exhibited an antimicrobial action that was dependent on the dose. Previous studies have revealed that CDs diminutive size and enhanced surface-to-volume ratio might induce membrane impairment or generate free radicals and oxidizing substances, causing bacterial killing [60]. Consequently, the toxicity of nanoparticles (NPs) towards microorganisms is influenced by factors such as their chemical makeup, size, the presence of functional groups on their surface, and the specific strains of bacteria involved [61]. CDs and bacteria usually first interact through direct physical interaction, which is facilitated by electrostatic reactions. This adhesion causes a physical and mechanical breakdown of the bacterial/fungal membrane, allowing CDs to penetrate the interiors. Generation of reactive oxygen species, binding of CDs to DNA, alteration in gene expressions, protein denaturation, enzyme disruption, and cell wall destruction all result in ion and cytoplasm leakage, which kills microorganisms (Fig. 4e) [62,63]. The small size of CDs has been shown to be directly correlated with the excellent antimicrobial activity [62]. Nevertheless, from a pharmaceutical perspective, a nano-antimicrobial agent with the ability to impact both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and fungal strains holds great importance and value. Future research should focus on mechanistic insights on the microbicidal activity of biomass-derived CDs.

The prepared MSTCDs showed excellent ability in detecting quercetin through fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence of MSTCDs (excitation = 360 nm) decreased when subjected to different amounts of quercetin (0 nM to 200 µM) and displayed a gradual redshift of the emission wavelength from 436.5 nm to 471.3 nm. At a concentration of 200 µM, the fluorescence of MSTCDs was significantly reduced by quercetin. The plot of F0/F vs. different amounts of quercetin showed a strong linear relationship in the range of 0.01–70 µM. The correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.99806, implying a significant amount of correlation, and the linear regression equation recorded was F0/F = 1.01424x + 0.01906 (Fig. 5b), which accurately describes the relationship between variables. The LOD of 2 nM was calculated using the formula 3*SD/slope, which is lower than the previous reports ( Table 2). The selectivity of MSTCDs for detecting quercetin was studied by checking the effect of various metal and non-metal ions, organic and inorganic compounds, polyphenol, and other flavanols on the PL intensity of MSTCDs. As shown in Fig. 6a, all the studied interferents did not significantly alter the fluorescence of MSTCDs. However, little quenching was observed in the presence of rutin, apigenin, tannic acid (polyphenol), and copper nitrate, but that was negligible in comparison to the quenching by quercetin. These results show the excellent selectivity and sensing capability of MSTCDs for quercetin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the quercetin sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensing stability of MSTCDs for quercetin, the effect on their fluorescence was checked by varying the pH levels from 3 to 14. As depicted in Fig. 6b, from pH 3–11, the MSTCDs showed excellent stability to detect quercetin [64]. The prepared MSTCDs were quite stable below pH 12. Hence, it was concluded that the MSTCDs exhibited high stability, rendering them potentially valuable for biological and sensing applications.

The phenomenon of fluorescence quenching arises from the collision between inhibitory and fluorescent particles and is commonly categorized into two distinct forms: dynamic and static quenching. The Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of F0/F against quercetin at different concentrations at varying temperatures was plotted (Fig. S7). A nonlinear graph was obtained with a positive deviation, suggesting that the quenching phenomenon is not solely attributed to collisions but may be influenced by the presence of simultaneous quenching mechanisms [65]. In addition, there was a noticeable shift in the absorption peak of MSTCDs, moving from 327 nm to 357.9 nm (Fig. 6c). This shift was credited to the creation of a ground-state complex, implying the presence of a static quenching mechanism [66].

Additionally, Fig. S8 shows no apparent variations in the life decay study results of MSTCDs before and after the inclusion of quercetin. The lifetime (τ) of MSTCDs was negligibly changed from 8.5 ns to 8.3 ns after the addition of quercetin, indicating that the quenching was due to static quenching [67].

Moreover, we plotted the absorption spectra of quercetin alongside the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MSTCDs to compare the relative positions of their spectra. There was a noticeable overlap in the spectra of the excitation peak of MSTCDs and the absorption peak of quercetin, as shown in Fig. S9. It further establishes that the quenching of MSTCDs is also a result of the inner filter effect (IFE), wherein quercetin blocks a portion of the excitation radiation of MSTCDs [68]. Consequently, the observed fluorescence intensity of MSTCDs was corrected using the given equation to showcase how IFE affects the reduction of fluorescence.

Wherein the variable Fobs denotes the measured fluorescence intensity, while the variable Fcorr represents the fluorescence intensity after subtracting the IFE. Aexc and Aemm correspond to the absorbance values of MSTCDs at 360 and 436.5 nm, respectively, after the addition of quercetin. The symbols d, s, and g represent the cuvette breadth (1 cm), excitation beam density (0.1 cm), and distance (0.4 cm) from the excitation beam's edge to the cuvette edge, respectively. Fluorescence quenching efficiencies were assessed at various quercetin concentrations, as indicated in Table S1, for the correction factor (CF) of the IFE (Fcor/Fobs), the observed percentage (Eobs%), and the corrected percentage (Ecor%). Fig. 6d illustrates the reduced efficiency (E%) of the Ecor and Eobs for MSTCDs in the presence of quercetin. Our findings indicate that the IFE quenching effectiveness of quercetin on MSTCDs can reach a maximum of 39 %, which implies the possibility of an additional quenching mechanism [69]. The molar extinction coefficient (ε) of quercetin and all interferents were estimated to obtain a more accurate understanding of the sensing of quercetin using IFE [70]. MSTCDs at an excitation of 360 nm depicted the highest ε of 3610 as shown in Table S2. This finding provides additional evidence supporting the role of IFE in quercetin detection. A previous study discovered a dual quenching mechanism that explains the interaction between quercetin and chemically synthesized CDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative measurement of quercetin using actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of quercetin in the actual samples was studied in order to assess the analytical reliability and possible applications of the suggested approach. The findings of the recovery of quercetin are presented in Table 3. The amount of quercetin naturally present in the orange and grape juices was found to be 7.04 and 3.27 µM, respectively. The spiked samples of orange juice, grape juice, and tap water with different amounts of quercetin (1, 20, and 60 µM) showed recoveries ranging from 99.9 % to 101.9 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) varying from 0.93 % to 1.59 %, depicting the effectiveness of this method in accurately detecting quercetin.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a fluorescent quercetin sensing probe called MSTCDs using a simple hydrothermal method with eco-friendly material. This sensor is highly reliable for detecting quercetin in orange juice, grape juice, and tap water, because of its larger linear range, excellent stability, sensitivity and selectivity. It was discovered that the reason behind the fluorescence quenching of MSTCDs caused by quercetin is due to the IFE and the occurrence of complexes in the ground state (static quenching mechanisms). Interestingly, the as-synthesized MSTCDs showed superb dose-dependent microbicidal activity against fungus, Gram-negative, and Gram-positive bacteria. These MSTCDs demonstrated promising potential as antioxidants, exhibiting good antiradical activity of 93.58, 84.34, and 70.16 % for ABTS, DPPH, and OH radicals, respectively. Thus, MSTCDs displayed excellent efficacy as quenching-based quercetin sensors as well as antioxidants and antimicrobial agents.
